perturbation.original_text: That I will not disclose any Information re garding these businesses to any other person who has not also signed and dated this agreement, except to secure their advice and counsel, in which case I agree to obtain their consent to maintain such confidentiality. “Information” shall include the f act that the business is for sale plus other data. The term Information does not include any information, which is, or becomes, generally available to the public or is already in your possession.
perturbation.changed_text: That I will not disclose any Information regarding these businesses to any other person who has not also signed and dated this agreement, except to secure their advice and counsel. “Information” shall include the fact that the business is for sale plus other data, even if that data becomes generally available to the public after the date of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The modification removes the exception for information that becomes publicly available. This broadens the definition of 'Information' under the contract beyond what is typically considered confidential under trade secret laws, creating a legal contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines a trade secret as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable. By including information that becomes publicly available within the definition of 'Information,' the contract attempts to protect data that does not qualify as a trade secret under the UTSA, thereby contradicting the established legal definition and scope of trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the definition of 'Information' to include data that becomes publicly available, directly contradicting the principle that trade secrets derive value from not being generally known. The Law Explanation highlights that the contract attempts to protect data that would not qualify as a trade secret, conflicting with established legal definitions. While the provided snippets are unhelpful, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation, combined with the altered definition in the Changed Text, clearly demonstrate that the contract now attempts to protect information outside the bounds of trade secret law, creating a legal contradiction as it redefines what constitutes confidential information beyond what is legally protected.

perturbation.original_text: That all Information is provided by the Seller and is not verified in any way by Tameracq Partners, Inc. Tameracq Partners, Inc is relying on Seller for the accuracy and completeness of said Information, has no knowle dge of the accuracy of said Information and makes no warranty, expressed or implied, as to such Information.
perturbation.changed_text: That all Information is provided by the Seller and is not verified in any way by Tameracq Partners, Inc. Tameracq Partners, Inc is relying on Seller for the accuracy and completeness of said Information and makes no warranty, expressed or implied, as to such Information. Tameracq Partners, Inc is not responsible for misrepresentations unless such misrepresentations are intentionally and fraudulently made.
perturbation.explanation: The added clause attempts to limit liability for misrepresentations to only cases of intentional fraud. This contradicts the common law and statutory standards for negligent misrepresentation, which do not always require intent.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Torts § 552 establishes liability for negligent misrepresentation, which occurs when a party provides false information without exercising reasonable care. The modified contract language attempts to limit liability to intentional misrepresentation, contradicting the broader scope of liability for negligent misrepresentation under common law.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract attempts to limit liability for misrepresentations to only those made intentionally and fraudulently. The Law Explanation states that the Restatement (Second) of Torts § 552 establishes liability for negligent misrepresentation, which occurs when a party provides false information without exercising reasonable care. The added clause therefore contradicts the broader scope of liability for negligent misrepresentation under common law because negligent misrepresentation does not always require intent, which is contradictory to the new text.

perturbation.original_text: That, prior to finalizing an agreement to purchase a busine ss, it is my responsibility to make an independent verification of all information. I agree that Tameracq Partners, Inc is not responsible for the accuracy of any Informati on I received and I agree to indemnify and hold Tameracq Partners, Inc harmless from any claims or damages resulting from its use.
perturbation.changed_text: That, prior to finalizing an agreement to purchase a business, it is my responsibility to make an independent verification of all information. I agree that Tameracq Partners, Inc is not responsible for the accuracy of any Information I received and I agree to indemnify and hold Tameracq Partners, Inc harmless from any claims or damages resulting from its use, regardless of whether Tameracq Partners, Inc acted negligently or intentionally.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'regardless of whether Tameracq Partners, Inc acted negligently or intentionally' attempts to indemnify the company even for its own negligence. This conflicts with many state laws that prohibit or severely restrict indemnification for one's own negligence.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Varies by state, e.g., California Civil Code § 2782
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 2782 generally invalidates indemnity agreements that purport to indemnify a party for their own sole negligence or willful misconduct. The added language in the contract attempts to circumvent this prohibition, thereby contradicting California law.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 12. INDEMNITY [2772 - 2784.5] ( Title 12 enacted 1872. ) 2782. (a) Except as provided in Sections 2782.1, 2782.2, 2782.5, and 2782.6, provisions, clauses, covenants, or agreements contained in, collateral to, or affecting any construction contract and that purport to indemnify the promisee against liability for damages for death or bodily injury to persons, injury to property, or any other loss, damage or expense arising from the sole negligence or willful misconduct of the promisee or the promisee’s agents, servants, or independent contractors who are directly responsible to the promisee, or for defects in design furnished by those persons, are against public policy and are void and unenforceable; provided, however, that this section shall not affect the validity of any insurance contract, workers’ compensation, or agreement issued by an admitted insurer as defined by the Insurance Code. (b) (1) Except as provided in Sections 2782.1, 2782.2, and 2782.5, provisions, clauses, covenants, or agreements contained in, collateral to, or affectin
perturbation.justification: The modified contract attempts to indemnify Tameracq Partners, Inc. even for its own negligence or intentional acts. This contradicts California Civil Code § 2782(a), as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which states that agreements purporting to indemnify a party against liability for damages arising from the 'sole negligence or willful misconduct of the promisee' are against public policy and void. The added phrase 'regardless of whether Tameracq Partners, Inc acted negligently or intentionally' directly attempts to circumvent this legal restriction, creating a direct conflict and making the contract clause unenforceable under California law.

perturbation.original_text: That, should I enter into an agreement to pur chase a business the Franchisee offers for sale, I grant to the Seller the right to obtain, through standard reporting agencies, financial and credit information concerning my self or the companies or other parties I represent and understand that this informa tion will be held conf idential by Seller and Tameracq Partners, Inc and will only be used for the purpose of Seller extending credit to me.
perturbation.changed_text: That, should I enter into an agreement to purchase a business the Franchisee offers for sale, I grant to the Seller the right to obtain, through standard reporting agencies, financial and credit information concerning myself or the companies or other parties I represent. This information may be shared with any third party deemed necessary by the Seller.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the confidentiality clause and adding the provision allowing the Seller to share the credit information with any third party contradicts the Fair Credit Reporting Act's (FCRA) limitations on the use and disclosure of credit information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA) limits the permissible purposes for which consumer credit information can be obtained and shared. By allowing the Seller to share this information with any third party, the modified contract violates the FCRA's restrictions on unauthorized disclosure of consumer credit information. 15 U.S.C. § 1681b outlines the permissible purposes for obtaining credit reports, and this open-ended sharing provision does not fall within those allowed purposes.

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the confidentiality clause and allows the Seller to share financial and credit information with any third party. This directly contradicts the Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA), which limits the permissible purposes for which consumer credit information can be obtained and shared. The original text ensured confidentiality and restricted use to extending credit, aligning with FCRA's intent. The modification allows unrestricted sharing, violating FCRA's limitations on unauthorized disclosure. Scraped Law Snippet 2 includes '1681b. Permissible purposes of consumer reports.', implying restrictions on who can access such data, which is undermined by the altered contract.
